groupProjects.name: Wind power production forecasting
groupProjects.descriptionProject: Family of models to predict electricity prices starting from available real-time data.

groupProjects.name: Web application
groupProjects.descriptionProject: Based on the public transport graphtraversal algorithm below, various web services were built and deployed: The winter map shows which winter sports areas can be reached from a certain place in a given amount of time. 2. The legacy map, which simply provides information on which public transport stops can be raeched from a given departure location within a given amount of time.

descriptionList.Data: Public transport graph descsribed below, complemented with information on the winter sports areas (winter map).
descriptionList.Challenges and lessons learned: Challenges: Not using a proper frontend framework; lessons learned: use a proper frontend framework. That said, the legacy map does work fairly well despite relying entirely on vanilla JS.
descriptionList.Outlook: While the winter map is a good starting point and easy to implement due to good data availability, future versions of this webservice could include a broad variety of touristic destinations.
descriptionList.Tech stack: Frontend:React (legacy map: Vanilla JS), Backend: Flask, Deployment: Docker, Nginx, gunicorn

groupProjects.name: GTFS graph expansion
groupProjects.descriptionProject: Python pipeline to expand GTFS datasets into an expanded format, i.e. a graph where nodes correspond to certain events in time (etc. bus/train arrivals/departures at certain public transport stop.)

descriptionList.Data: Any standard format GTFS dataset; for the projects described here, the framework is applied to the comprehensive dataset of the very extensive Swiss public transport system.

groupProjects.name: Graph algorithm
groupProjects.descriptionProject: A single-source shortest distance graph algorithm. The GTFS graph defined earlier is traversed in order to find all stops in order to .

descriptionList.Data: The expanded GTFS graph stored in a graph database.
descriptionList.Model and implementation:
Thanks to the structure of the graph, a simple single-source shortest distance algorithm automatically generates valid connections. The algorithm is implemented as part of a Java Spring API. Thanks to this design choice, the graph remains in memory as native Java objects, allowing for repeated traversal without I/O overhead.
In principle, available packages for single-source breadth-first-searches could be used. However, implementing a dedicated algorithm for the sake of this project has a couple of advantages:
  1. Fine-tuning of traversal: For example, if two connections have the exact same duration, we prefer the one with fewer changes
  2. Better integration with the server architecture:
  3. Full control over the route information that's being stored: It turns out to be most efficient to directly store all information on the route during the traversal. Implementing a custom algorithm enables full control over what information is saved for this purpose

The expanded public transport network is traversed in order to reproduce the connections as suggested e.g. by the Swiss railway company.
descriptionList.Impact: This implementation of the single-source shortest-distance algorithm is the backbone of the web services. Further, it is immensely satisfying to
descriptionList.Challenges and lessons learned: Previous versions implemented the graph as custom Neo4J proccedure or made use of the internal Neo4J Java API in other ways. This caused database I/O bottlenecks. The current implementation relies on an in-memory representation of the graph as native Java objects which is fully loaded once on server startup.
descriptionList.Outlook:
Adaptations of the algorithm for other use cases is straight-forward. For example:
  • Focus on changes: Which stops can be reached with direct connections or with few changes?
  • Reversed direction: From which other places can a destination be reached within a certain amount of time?

groupProjects.name: Power market
groupProjects.descriptionProject: The <u>G</u>ene<u>R</u>al <u>I</u>ntegrated <u>M</u>odeling framework for the <u>S</u>upply of <u>E</u>lectricity and <u>L</u>ow-temperature heat ("Grimsel") was the main foundation for the research underlying my PhD research.

descriptionList.Data: The Grimsel input consists in a normalized relational database defining the systems' energy transformers, consumers, and storage capacities.
descriptionList.Impact: Grimsel was used as the research backbone of multiple peer-reviewed publications, both by myself and by fellow researchers. While I am not involved in the development and maintenance of the library and longer, it remains in active use.
descriptionList.Implementation:
Built on top of Python's general optimization library Pyomo, Grimsel facilitates the definition of energy models along their whole life cycle:
  1. Enforcement of input data structure.
  2. Translation of input data to model structure (constraints, parameters, variables) and parameters.
  3. Tools for easy modification of the model to facilitate studies based on extensive parameter variations.
  4. Streamlined output to various formats.
The scope of Grimsel was kept deliberately general: From the start it was designed to support a broad range of energy system structures, including arbitrariy conversions of energy carriers. While my personal research was limited to electricity only, this violation of the YAGNI-principle turned out to be highly beneficial for śubsequent research work encompassing both heat and electricity.
descriptionList.Lessons learned: The open source space provided multiple other packages and frameworks with similar scope. Was it the best choice to write another one instead of contributing to an existing framework? Likely not. However, building Grimsel from scratch enabled its streamlined adaptation to the research questions at hand.

groupProjects.name: Data scraping
groupProjects.descriptionProject: A bundle of Python libraries to retrieve power data from various sources.

descriptionList.Data sources: A broad range of openly accessible online data sources in more or less exotic formats.
descriptionList.Model and implementation: A Python module to parse a broad range of time series from semi-structured data sources. Methods ranged from standard web scraping using Python/Beautifulsoup to more involved data extraction from pdf or svg files. All data were harmonized (UTC!) and stored in a normalized database. Somewhere in the pipeline there is also a script to identify timezones based on time-shifted cross-correlations.

descriptionList.Tech stack: Backend: Python (beautifulsoup, pandas), postgresql; front-end: streamlit; deployment: Heroku

groupProjects.name: SymEnergy modeling framework
groupProjects.descriptionProject: Economic energy models approximating markets through mathematical optimization often exhibit complex behaviors which are challenging to interpret. SymEnergy enables defining stylized <it>symbolic</it> models, in the sense that the focus lies on solutions in the form of closed-form expressions (variables as functions of parameters) rather than numerical values.

descriptionList.Data: None. Due to the stylized nature of the models (e.g. two to four time slots), no input data is used. Instead, we might define a model with two to four time slots and two to three power or storage plants and investigate the behavior by systematically varying renewable power production during one of the time slots.
descriptionList.Model and implementation: SymEnergy is a Python module which relies heavily on the Sympy symbolic maths library. Most importantly, Sympy is used to solve the linear equations resulting from the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions to optimize the energy systems' operation. A challenge in the implementation of this brute-force Lagrange approach consists in the management of the binding and non-binding constraints. Even for small systems, the numbers of combinations of constraints reach very large numbers.
descriptionList.Outlook and lessons learned:
The SymPy was used in the context of multiple academic publications and is currently unmaintained.
Lessons learned include the
descriptionList.Tech stack: SymPy with the support of Pandas. In addition, the package makes extensive use of the python built-in multiprocessing module for exploitation of embarassing parallelism when calculating the models' solutions.

groupProjects.name: Storage disaggregation
groupProjects.descriptionProject: Charging/discharging profiles of energy storage can exhibit complex periodicity, depending on the underlying drivers of storage operation. For example, diurnal pumped hydro power operation is primarily driven by hours of high (noon) and low (night time) demand. However, for increasing solar power generation, pumping at noon becomes more relevant. This raises the question, on which time scales (4 hours? 12 hours?) the storage is operating and for how many hours the energy is stored. groupProjects.name: Data story (work in progress)
groupProjects.descriptionProject: General public knowledge on the state of global warming is insufficient. The often cited average global temperature increase since pre-industrial times gives a misleading impression with respect to warming trends' diversity in different parts of the globe. This project is envisioned to result in a scrollable data story illustrating this diversity. Key takeaways are the much more rapid warming in the polar regions and the differences differences between land and sea. Development stalled due to a shift in focus away from front-end development. There will be spinning globes.

descriptionList.Data: Geographically gridded temperature time series with 0.25° resolution ranging back to 1950 were retrieved from the ECMWF/Copernicus. They were complemented with Natural Earth political and landmass border vector data for filtering.
descriptionList.Model and implementation: Data analysis made use of . A rough technical draft of the still to be published, visually appealing data story has been implemented.

descriptionList.Data: Data with X resolution (XX format) with XX time resolution ranging back to 19XX was retrieved from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts
descriptionList.Tech stack: Data analysis: Python (geopandas), ... ; data story: vanilla JS, d3.js
